title: Rice University Joins DOE Quantum Science Center to Advance Real-Time Quantum Error Correction - Quantum Computing Report
author: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
text:
Rice University Joins DOE Quantum Science Center to Advance Real-Time Quantum Error Correction

Rice University has officially joined the U.S. Department of Energy’s (DOE) Quantum Science Center (QSC), expanding its footprint in national fault-tolerant quantum computing R&D. Headquartered at Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL), the QSC is one of five National Quantum Information Science Research Centers established under the National Quantum Initiative Act. Led by Tirthak Patel, assistant professor of computer science at Rice, the university’s research group will receive approximately $900,000 over five years from the center. The project will focus on developing, optimizing, and evaluating quantum error-correction (QEC) decoding algorithms engineered to run directly on classical High-Performance Computing (HPC) supercomputing architectures.

Targeting Real-Time Latency and Scalable QEC Decoders

Physical quantum processors require continuous error detection and correction to maintain logical qubits. However, extracting syndrome data, processing error-decoding algorithms, and applying active corrections must occur within sub-microsecond physical limits. If classical decoding latency exceeds physical qubit coherence times, logical gate execution fails.

Patel’s team at Rice is contributing directly to the QSC’s Quantum-Accelerated High-Performance Computing (QHPC) Controls Project, collaborating closely with project lead Jack Lange at ORNL:

Hierarchical Decoding Frameworks: Designing scalable, multi-tier decoding algorithms that can be distributed across heterogeneous HPC architectures.

System-Level Bottleneck Remediation: Evaluating data movement, signal extraction, throughput, and communication latency between quantum processing units (QPUs) and classical HPC control nodes.

Multi-Platform QEC Evaluation: Testing error-correction pipeline performance across diverse quantum hardware backends to evaluate different fault-tolerant technological pathways.

Supporting the DOE’s 2028 Fault-Tolerant Ecosystem Vision

The integration of Rice University follows the DOE’s multi-year renewal of the Quantum Science Center through 2030, backed by a planned $125 million allocation over five years. According to QSC Director Travis Humble, integrating Rice’s systems-level computer science expertise directly addresses critical engineering constraints as the DOE works toward its goal of establishing a functional, fault-tolerant quantum computing ecosystem by 2028. By combining Rice’s algorithm optimization capabilities with ORNL’s exascale computing infrastructure, the partnership aims to accelerate the deployment of hybrid QHPC platforms for materials science, energy grid modeling, and chemistry research.

Review the official announcement on Rice News here.

July 22, 2026
